title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read an article from which six sentences have been removed. Choose from the sentences A–G the one which fits each gap (37–42). There is one extra sentence which you do not need to use.

content.title: Castles in the Sand: The World of Competitive Sand Sculpting
content.text: On a beach in the Netherlands, a team of sculptors is transforming a twenty-tonne block of compacted sand into a detailed replica of a medieval city, complete with towers, bridges and tiny market stalls. They have been working for four days and have three more to go. This is competitive sand sculpting at the highest level. (37) [37] Top competitors spend weeks planning their designs and can produce structures of extraordinary detail and scale. [/37] || The sand itself is the most important variable. Sculptors cannot work with beach sand straight from the shore — the grains are too uniformly rounded to stick together. Competition-grade sand has angular grains that interlock, and it is mixed with water and compacted into blocks using wooden forms. (38) [38] Too wet and the sand collapses; too dry and it crumbles under the sculptor's tools. [/38] Getting this mixture right is itself a skill developed over years. || The tools of the trade are surprisingly simple: metal spatulas, loop tools similar to those used by pottery makers, and small brushes for adding fine detail. (39) [39] The finest surface textures — individual roof tiles, fabric folds, facial expressions — are created with nothing more elaborate than a stick or a fingernail. [/39] Many sculptors refuse to use more complex equipment, valuing the directness of hand-to-material contact. || Team events add another layer of complexity. Large installations typically require between two and eight sculptors working in close coordination. (40) [40] Roles within the team must be clearly defined from the outset, with some members roughing out the large forms and others applying fine detail. [/40] || Weather is the sculptor's constant adversary. High winds can strip surface detail; rain can undermine structural integrity; direct sun dries the outer surface unevenly. (41) [41] Experienced competitors check weather forecasts obsessively and adjust their working schedule around predicted conditions. [/41] || When a competition ends, the sculptures are left to the elements. Unlike marble or bronze, sand does not endure. (42) [42] This impermanence is, for many practitioners, the philosophy at the heart of the art — beauty created fully in the knowledge that it will not last. [/42]

paragraphs.A: Protecting finished areas from the wind while continuing to work on others is one of the great logistical challenges of the craft.
paragraphs.B: The right water content is critical and must be maintained throughout the sculpting process.
paragraphs.C: The tide that erases a week's work in an hour is accepted as the natural end of every piece.
paragraphs.D: Despite their apparent simplicity, each of these tools requires years of practice to master.
paragraphs.E: Disagreements over design or execution can destroy team cohesion just as surely as bad weather can destroy a sculpture.
paragraphs.F: It is a medium that rewards patience, problem-solving and an ability to visualise a finished form within a featureless block.
paragraphs.G: Protective sprays can harden the surface against light rain, but no coating can withstand a serious downpour.

questions.explanation: F describes the medium as one that rewards patience, problem-solving and the ability to visualise, supporting the introduction about the planning and scale of the competitors.
B indicates that the correct water content is critical and must be maintained throughout the process, connecting with the description of the effects of having the mixture too wet or dry.
D notes that despite their apparent simplicity, each tool requires years of practice, supporting the description of the simple tools used.
E warns that disagreements can destroy team cohesion just as bad weather destroys a sculpture, supporting the need for coordination and defined roles.
G mentions protective sprays and their limitations in heavy rain, expanding the strategies for dealing with adverse weather conditions.
C accepts the tide that wipes out the work in an hour as the natural end of each piece, supporting the philosophy of impermanence described at the end of the article.
